Takeaway
If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened.
If we had documented the near miss, we would not be here now.
If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.
If we had logged it, we would already know the fix.
Ana, I read your postmortem for the outage. Before we share it, can you tell me the single most important cause?
Sure. If we had tested the new login flow in staging, we would not have shipped the broken build. The outage was preventable.
Agreed. And you called the switched-off alert a contributing factor, not the root cause. Why the difference?
Because the missing check was the root cause. The alert was an oversight that made it worse, but it did not cause the bug by itself.
That is fair. There was also a near miss last sprint, right? Does that change anything now?
It does. If we had documented that near miss, we would not be here now. The same problem came back because nobody wrote it down.
Understood. And you kept the whole thing blameless. Was that on purpose?
Completely. A postmortem can backfire if it turns into blame. In hindsight, the honest takeaway is simple: test every login change.
If we had documented the near miss, we would not be dealing with it now.
This is a blameless postmortem: we look at causes, not at people.
In hindsight, that oversight was a contributing factor.

Hi Ana, it is David from the platform team. I just read the postmortem you wrote for last week's outage, and honestly, it is one of the clearest I have seen. I have two quick questions before we share it with the wider team. First, you wrote that the outage was preventable, and that if we had tested the login flow in staging, it would not have happened. I completely agree, but should we call the old, switched-off alert the root cause, or just a contributing factor? I want us to be precise. Second, you mentioned a near miss in the previous sprint that nobody logged. Do you think we should add a separate action item just for that, a place to write down near misses, or is that already covered by the new staging test? One more thing: I really liked that this stayed blameless. Nobody felt attacked, and that is exactly why people were honest about what went wrong. Send me two short lines and I will forward it. Thanks, Ana.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on running postmortems, because yours are getting really strong. The secret is to keep them blameless and to use hindsight well. When you look back, do not say someone should have known. Instead, use a conditional: if we had tested this in staging, the outage would not have happened. That moves the focus from a person to a process. And here is the part people forget: mixed conditionals are your friend in a retrospective. When a past mistake is still hurting you today, say if we had documented that near miss, we would not be dealing with it now. It connects the past cause to the present pain, and it makes the takeaway obvious. One more thing: be careful, because a postmortem can backfire. The moment it feels like blame, people go quiet and hide the next incident. So always separate the cause from the person, find the contributing factors, agree on preventable actions, and let the team leave feeling safe. That is what makes your reviews so easy to trust.

Here is how Ana ran the postmortem after the outage. First, she wrote a factual timeline of the incident, from the first alert to the fix. Then she found the root cause and the contributing factors, without blaming anyone. After that, she used hindsight to see what the team would have done differently. Next, she agreed on preventable actions so the same outage would not happen again. Finally, she shared the key takeaways in a calm, blameless retrospective.
